title: Anti-Stalinist left

infobox.value: encompasses various kinds of [left-wing](/wiki/Left-wing_politics "Left-wing politics") political movements that oppose [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin"), [Stalinism](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism"), [neo-Stalinism](/wiki/Neo-Stalinism "Neo-Stalinism") and the [system of governance that Stalin implemented](/wiki/History_of_the_Soviet_Union_(1927%E2%80%931953) "History of the Soviet Union (1927–1953)") as leader of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") between 1924 and 1953. This term also refers to those that opposed Joseph Stalin and his leadership from within the [Communist movement](/wiki/Commu

The **anti-Stalinist left** encompasses various kinds of [left-wing](/wiki/Left-wing_politics "Left-wing politics") political movements that oppose [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin"), [Stalinism](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism"), [neo-Stalinism](/wiki/Neo-Stalinism "Neo-Stalinism") and the [system of governance that Stalin implemented](/wiki/History_of_the_Soviet_Union_(1927%E2%80%931953) "History of the Soviet Union (1927–1953)") as leader of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") between 1924 and 1953. This term also refers to those that opposed Joseph Stalin and his leadership from within the [Communist movement](/wiki/Communist_movement "Communist movement"), such as [Leon Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky") and the party's [Left Opposition](/wiki/Left_Opposition "Left Opposition").

In recent years, the term may also refer to left and centre-left wing opposition to [dictatorship](/wiki/Dictatorship "Dictatorship"), [cult of personality](/wiki/Cult_of_personality "Cult of personality"), [totalitarianism](/wiki/Totalitarianism "Totalitarianism") and [police states](/wiki/Police_state "Police state"), all being features commonly attributed to [Marxist–Leninist](/wiki/Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism "Marxism–Leninism") regimes that took inspiration from Stalinism such as the regimes of [Kim Il Sung](/wiki/Kim_Il_Sung "Kim Il Sung"), [Enver Hoxha](/wiki/Enver_Hoxha "Enver Hoxha") and others, including in the former [Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) Some of the notable movements within the anti-Stalinist left have been [Trotskyism](/wiki/Trotskyism "Trotskyism") and [Titoism](/wiki/Titoism "Titoism"), [anarchism](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism") and [libertarian socialism](/wiki/Libertarian_socialism "Libertarian socialism"), [left communism](/wiki/Left_communism "Left communism") and [libertarian Marxism](/wiki/Libertarian_Marxism "Libertarian Marxism"), the [Right Opposition](/wiki/Right_Opposition "Right Opposition") within the Communist movement, [Eurocommunism](/wiki/Eurocommunism "Eurocommunism"), [ultra-leftism](/wiki/Ultra-leftism "Ultra-leftism"), [democratic socialism](/wiki/Democratic_socialism "Democratic socialism") and [social democracy](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy").

## Revolutionary era critiques (pre-1924)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anti-Stalinist_left&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Revolutionary era critiques (pre-1924)")]

Main articles: [Left-wing uprisings against the Bolsheviks](/wiki/Left-wing_uprisings_against_the_Bolsheviks "Left-wing uprisings against the Bolsheviks"), [Kronstadt rebellion](/wiki/Kronstadt_rebellion "Kronstadt rebellion"), [Left communism](/wiki/Left_communism "Left communism"), and [libertarian Marxism](/wiki/Libertarian_Marxism "Libertarian Marxism")

A large majority of the political left was initially enthusiastic about the [Bolshevik Revolution](/wiki/Bolshevik_Revolution "Bolshevik Revolution") in the revolutionary era. In the beginning, the [Bolsheviks](/wiki/Bolsheviks "Bolsheviks") and their policies received much support because the movement was originally painted by Lenin and other leaders in a [libertarian](/wiki/Libertarian "Libertarian") light.[[4]](#cite_note-Linkhoeva-4)[[5]](#cite_note-b354-5)[[6]](#cite_note-:12-6) However, as more politically repressive methods were used, the Bolsheviks steadily lost support from many anarchists and revolutionaries.[[7]](#cite_note-Swain-7)[[6]](#cite_note-:12-6)[[4]](#cite_note-Linkhoeva-4) Prominent [anarchist communists](/wiki/Anarcho-communism "Anarcho-communism") and [libertarian Marxists](/wiki/Libertarian_Marxism "Libertarian Marxism") such as [Sylvia Pankhurst](/wiki/Sylvia_Pankhurst "Sylvia Pankhurst"),[[8]](#cite_note-d708-8) [Rosa Luxemburg](/wiki/Rosa_Luxemburg#Criticism_of_the_October_Revolution "Rosa Luxemburg"),[[9]](#cite_note-9) and [Emma Goldman](/wiki/Emma_Goldman "Emma Goldman")[[6]](#cite_note-:12-6) were among the first left-wing critics of [Bolshevism](/wiki/Bolshevism "Bolshevism").

Rosa Luxemburg was heavily critical of the methods that Bolsheviks used to seize power in the [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution") claiming that it was "not a movement of the people but of the bourgeoisie".[[10]](#cite_note-:4-10) Primarily, Luxemburg's critiques were based on the manner in which the Bolsheviks suppressed anarchist movements.[[11]](#cite_note-11) In one of her essays published titled "The Nationalities Question in the Russian Revolution", she explains:[[10]](#cite_note-:4-10)

> To be sure, in all these cases, it was really not the "people" who engaged in these reactionary policies, but only the bourgeois and petit bourgeois classes, who – in sharpest opposition to their own proletarian masses – perverted the "national right of [self-determination](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination")" into an instrument of their counter-revolutionary class policies.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-14077-006%2C_Rosa_Luxemburg.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-14077-006%2C_Rosa_Luxemburg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-14077-006,_Rosa_Luxemburg.jpg)

Rosa Luxemburg's political legacy was criticized by Stalin after he rose to power.

Because of her early criticisms toward the Bolsheviks, her legacy was vilified by Stalin once he rose to power.[[12]](#cite_note-:5-12) According to Trotsky, Stalin was "often lying about her and vilifying her" in the eyes of the public.[[12]](#cite_note-:5-12)

The relations between the anarchists and the Bolsheviks worsened in Soviet Russia due to the suppression of movements like the [Kronstadt rebellion](/wiki/Kronstadt_rebellion "Kronstadt rebellion") and the [Makhnovist](/wiki/Makhnovist "Makhnovist") movement.[[7]](#cite_note-Swain-7)[[13]](#cite_note-Zimmer-13)[[6]](#cite_note-:12-6) The Kronstadt rebellion (March 1921) was a key moment during which many libertarian and democratic leftists broke with the Bolsheviks, laying the foundations for the anti-Stalinist left. The American anti-Stalinist socialist [Daniel Bell](/wiki/Daniel_Bell "Daniel Bell") later said:

> Every radical generation, it is said, has its Kronstadt. For some it was the [Moscow Trials](/wiki/Moscow_Trials "Moscow Trials"), for others the [Nazi-Soviet Pact](/wiki/Nazi-Soviet_Pact "Nazi-Soviet Pact"), for still others Hungary (The [Raik Trial](/wiki/L%C3%A1szl%C3%B3_Rajk "László Rajk") or [1956](/wiki/Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956 "Hungarian Revolution of 1956")), Czechoslovakia (the defenestration of [Masaryk](/wiki/Jan_Masaryk "Jan Masaryk") in 1948 or the [Prague Spring](/wiki/Prague_Spring "Prague Spring") of 1968), the [Gulag](/wiki/Gulag "Gulag"), [Cambodia](/wiki/Cambodian_genocide "Cambodian genocide"), [Poland](/wiki/Martial_law_in_Poland "Martial law in Poland") (and there will be more to come). My Kronstadt was Kronstadt.[[14]](#cite_note-Bell-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Hitchens_2008-15)[[16]](#cite_note-UC_Press_E-Books_Collection,_1982-2004-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Dissent_Magazine_2012-17)

Another key anti-Stalinist, [Louis Fischer](/wiki/Louis_Fischer "Louis Fischer"), later coined the term "Kronstadt moment" for this.[[15]](#cite_note-Hitchens_2008-15)

Like Rosa Luxemburg, Emma Goldman was primarily critical of [Lenin's style of leadership](/wiki/Leninism "Leninism"), but her focus eventually transferred over to Stalin and [his policies](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism") as he rose to power.[[18]](#cite_note-Wexler-18)[[6]](#cite_note-:12-6) In her essay titled "There Is No Communism in Russia", Goldman details how Stalin "abused the power of his position" and formed a dictatorship.[[6]](#cite_note-:12-6) In this text she states:[[6]](#cite_note-:12-6)

> In other words, by the Central Committee and Politbureau of the Party, both of them controlled absolutely by one man, Stalin. To call such a dictatorship, this personal autocracy more powerful and absolute than any Czar's, by the name of Communism seems to me the acme of imbecility.

Emma Goldman asserted that there was "not the least sign in Soviet Russia even of authoritarian, State Communism".[[6]](#cite_note-:12-6) Emma Goldman remained critical of Stalin and the Bolshevik's style of governance up until her death in 1940.[[19]](#cite_note-:6-19)

Overall, the left communists and anarchists were critical of the statist, repressive, and [totalitarian](/wiki/Totalitarian "Totalitarian") nature of [Marxism–Leninism](/wiki/Marxism%E2%80%93Leninism "Marxism–Leninism") which eventually carried over to Stalinism and Stalin's policy in general.[[19]](#cite_note-:6-19) Conversely, Trotsky argued that he and Lenin had intended to lift the ban on the [opposition parties](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_the_Soviet_Union "List of political parties in the Soviet Union") such as the [Mensheviks](/wiki/Mensheviks "Mensheviks") and [Socialist Revolutionaries](/wiki/Socialist_Revolutionaries "Socialist Revolutionaries") as soon as the economic and social conditions of [Soviet Russia](/wiki/Soviet_Russia "Soviet Russia") had improved.[[20]](#cite_note-books.google.com-20)

## After Stalin's rise to power (1924–1930)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anti-Stalinist_left&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: After Stalin's rise to power (1924–1930)")]

Main articles: [Old Bolsheviks](/wiki/Old_Bolsheviks "Old Bolsheviks"), [Left Opposition](/wiki/Left_Opposition "Left Opposition"), [United Opposition (Soviet Union)](/wiki/United_Opposition_(Soviet_Union) "United Opposition (Soviet Union)"), [Right Opposition](/wiki/Right_Opposition "Right Opposition"), and [Third Period](/wiki/Third_Period "Third Period")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/dd/Nikolai_Bukharin.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Nikolai_Bukharin.jpeg)

A young Nikolai Bukharin, whose ideas formed the ideological framework of the Right Opposition

> He is an unprincipled intriguer, who subordinates everything to the preservation of his own power. He changes his theory according to whom he needs to get rid of.

Bukharin on Stalin's theoretical position, 1928.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The struggle for power in the Soviet Union after the death of [Vladimir Lenin](/wiki/Vladimir_Lenin "Vladimir Lenin") in 1924 saw the development of three major tendencies within the [All-Union Communist Party (Bolsheviks)](/wiki/All-Union_Communist_Party_(Bolsheviks) "All-Union Communist Party (Bolsheviks)"). These were described by Trotsky as left, right, and centre tendencies. The [Right Opposition](/wiki/Right_Opposition "Right Opposition") was a label formulated by Stalin in Autumn of 1928 for the opposition against certain measures included within the [first five-year plan](/wiki/First_five-year_plan_(Soviet_Union) "First five-year plan (Soviet Union)"), an opposition which was led by [Nikolai Bukharin](/wiki/Nikolai_Bukharin "Nikolai Bukharin"), [Alexei Rykov](/wiki/Alexei_Rykov "Alexei Rykov"), [Mikhail Tomsky](/wiki/Mikhail_Tomsky "Mikhail Tomsky"), and their supporters within the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") that did not follow the so-called "[general line of the party](/wiki/General_line_of_the_party "General line of the party")". Stalin and his "centre" faction were allied with Bukharin and the Right Opposition from late 1924, with Bukharin elaborating Stalin's theory of [socialism in one country](/wiki/Socialism_in_one_country "Socialism in one country"). Together, they expelled Trotsky, Kamenev, Zinoviev, and the [United Opposition](/wiki/United_Opposition_(Soviet_Union) "United Opposition (Soviet Union)") from the [Communist Party](/wiki/All-Union_Communist_Party_(Bolsheviks) "All-Union Communist Party (Bolsheviks)") in December 1927. However, once Trotsky was out of the way and the Left Opposition had been illegalized, Stalin soon turned on his Right Opposition allies. Bukharin and the Right Opposition were, in their turn, sidelined and removed from important positions within the Communist Party and the Soviet government from 1928 to 1930, with Stalin ending the NEP and beginning the first [five-year plan](/wiki/Five-year_plans_for_the_national_economy_of_the_Soviet_Union "Five-year plans for the national economy of the Soviet Union").

One of the last attempts of the Right Opposition to resist Stalin was the [Ryutin affair](/wiki/Ryutin_affair "Ryutin affair") in 1932, where a manifesto against the policy of [collectivization](/wiki/Collectivization "Collectivization") was circulated; it openly called for "The Liquidation of the dictatorship of Stalin and his clique".[[22]](#cite_note-22) Later, some rightists joined a [secret bloc](/wiki/Bloc_of_Soviet_Oppositions "Bloc of Soviet Oppositions") with [Leon Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky"), [Zinoviev](/wiki/Grigory_Zinoviev "Grigory Zinoviev") and [Kamenev](/wiki/Lev_Kamenev "Lev Kamenev") to oppose Stalin. Historian [Pierre Broué](/wiki/Pierre_Brou%C3%A9 "Pierre Broué") stated that it dissolved in early 1933.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Leon_Trotsky%2C_1930s.jpg/250px-Leon_Trotsky%2C_1930s.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leon_Trotsky,_1930s.jpg)

[Bolshevik](/wiki/Bolsheviks "Bolsheviks") revolutionary [Leon Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky") was exiled by Stalin in February 1929.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Trotsky would become the most vocal and prominent critic of [Stalinism](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism") in the early 20th century.

[Leon Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky") and Stalin disagreed on issues of [industrialization](/wiki/Industrialization_in_the_Soviet_Union "Industrialization in the Soviet Union") and revolutionary tactics.[[25]](#cite_note-:72-25) Trotsky believed that there was a need for super-industrialization while Stalin believed in a rapid surge and [collectivization](/wiki/Collectivization "Collectivization"), as written in his [5-year plan](/wiki/Five-year_plans_of_the_Soviet_Union "Five-year plans of the Soviet Union").[[25]](#cite_note-:72-25) Trotsky believed an accelerated global surge to be the answer to institute communism globally.[[25]](#cite_note-:72-25) Trotsky was critical of Stalin's methods because he believed the slower pace of collectivization and industrialization to be ineffective in the long run.[[25]](#cite_note-:72-25) According to historian [Sheila Fitzpatrick](/wiki/Sheila_Fitzpatrick "Sheila Fitzpatrick"), the scholarly consensus is that Stalin appropriated the position of the [Left Opposition](/wiki/Left_Opposition "Left Opposition") on such matters as [industrialisation](/wiki/Industrialisation "Industrialisation") and [collectivisation](/wiki/Collectivisation "Collectivisation").[[26]](#cite_note-26) Trotsky also disagreed with Stalin's thesis of [Socialism in One Country](/wiki/Socialism_in_One_Country "Socialism in One Country"),[[25]](#cite_note-:72-25) believing that the institution of revolution in one state or country would not be as effective as a global revolution.[[27]](#cite_note-:23-27) He also criticized how the Socialism in One Country thesis broke with the [internationalist](/wiki/Proletarian_internationalism "Proletarian internationalism") traditions of [Marxism](/wiki/Marxism "Marxism").[[28]](#cite_note-:33-28) Trotskyists believed that a permanent global revolution was the most effective method to ensure the system of communism was enacted worldwide.[[27]](#cite_note-:23-27) According to his biographer, [Isaac Deutscher](/wiki/Isaac_Deutscher "Isaac Deutscher"), Trotsky explicitly supported [proletarian internationalism](/wiki/Proletarian_internationalism "Proletarian internationalism") but was opposed to achieving this via [military](/wiki/Military "Military") [conquest](/wiki/Conquest "Conquest") as seen with his documented opposition to the [war with Poland](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Soviet_War "Polish–Soviet War") in 1920, proposed armistice with the [Entente](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") and temperance with staging [anti-British revolts](/wiki/British_foreign_policy_in_the_Middle_East "British foreign policy in the Middle East") in the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East").[[29]](#cite_note-29) Overall, Trotsky and his followers were very critical of the lack of internal debate and discussion among Stalinist organizations along with their politically [repressive methods](/wiki/Political_repression_in_the_Soviet_Union "Political repression in the Soviet Union").[[27]](#cite_note-:23-27)[[28]](#cite_note-:33-28)

## The consolidation of Stalin's rule and responding to the rise of fascism in Europe (1930–1939)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anti-Stalinist_left&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: The consolidation of Stalin's rule and responding to the rise of fascism in Europe (1930–1939)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Three_Arrows_election_poster_of_the_Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany%2C_1932_-_Gegen_Papen%2C_Hitler%2C_Th%C3%A4lmann.png/250px-Three_Arrows_election_poster_of_the_Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany%2C_1932_-_Gegen_Papen%2C_Hitler%2C_Th%C3%A4lmann.png)](/wiki/File:Three_Arrows_election_poster_of_the_Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany,_1932_-_Gegen_Papen,_Hitler,_Th%C3%A4lmann.png)

A widely publicized election poster of the [Social Democratic Party of Germany](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") from 1932, with the [Three Arrows](/wiki/Three_Arrows "Three Arrows") symbol representing resistance against [reactionary](/wiki/Reactionary "Reactionary") conservatism, [Nazism](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism") and [Stalinism](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism"), alongside the slogan "Against [Papen](/wiki/Franz_von_Papen "Franz von Papen"), [Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler"), [Thälmann](/wiki/Ernst_Th%C3%A4lmann "Ernst Thälmann")"

Main articles: [Great Purge](/wiki/Great_Purge "Great Purge"), [Moscow Trials](/wiki/Moscow_Trials "Moscow Trials"), [Dewey Commission](/wiki/Dewey_Commission "Dewey Commission"), [Fourth International](/wiki/Fourth_International "Fourth International"), [Spanish Revolution of 1936](/wiki/Spanish_Revolution_of_1936 "Spanish Revolution of 1936"), and [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War")

During the 1930s, critics of Stalin, both inside and outside the Soviet Union, were under heavy attack by the party. According to historian, [Bernhard H. Bayerlein](/wiki/Bernhard_H._Bayerlein "Bernhard H. Bayerlein"), the increasingly "repressive transformation" of the Communist movement "strengthened intermediate oppositionist and anti-Stalinist currents” in the left.[[30]](#cite_note-Bayerlein-30)

Outside the Communist movement, for example, the [International Revolutionary Marxist Centre](/wiki/International_Revolutionary_Marxist_Centre "International Revolutionary Marxist Centre") was founded in 1932 as an international association of left-wing parties which rejected both more moderate mainstream [social democracy](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy") and the Stalinist [Third International](/wiki/Third_International "Third International").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Diego_rivera_Commies.jpg/250px-Diego_rivera_Commies.jpg)](/wiki/File:Diego_rivera_Commies.jpg)

A [Diego Rivera](/wiki/Diego_Rivera "Diego Rivera") mural (*[Man, Controller of the Universe](/wiki/Man,_Controller_of_the_Universe "Man, Controller of the Universe")*) depicts Trotsky with Marx and Engels as a true champion of the workers' struggle.

While defending the Russian Revolution from outside aggression, [Leon Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky") and his followers at the same time urged an anti-bureaucratic [political revolution](/wiki/Political_revolution_(Trotskyism) "Political revolution (Trotskyism)") against [Stalinism](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism") to be conducted by the Soviet working class themselves. In 1936, Trotsky called for the restoration of the right of criticism in areas such as [economic matters](/wiki/Economic_policy "Economic policy"), the revitalization of the [trade unions](/wiki/Trade_unions "Trade unions") and the free elections of the [Soviet parties](/wiki/List_of_political_parties_in_the_Soviet_Union "List of political parties in the Soviet Union").[[31]](#cite_note-31) Trotsky also opposed the policy of forced collectivisation under Stalin and favoured a [voluntary](/wiki/Volunteering "Volunteering"), gradual approach towards [agricultural production](/wiki/Collective_farming "Collective farming")[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) with greater tolerance for the rights of [Soviet Ukrainians](/wiki/Ukrainian_Soviet_Socialist_Republic "Ukrainian Soviet Socialist Republic").[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35) From 1936, Trotsky and his American supporter [James P. Cannon](/wiki/James_P._Cannon "James P. Cannon") described the Soviet Union as a "[degenerated workers' state](/wiki/Degenerated_workers%27_state "Degenerated workers' state")", the revolutionary gains of which should be defended against imperialist aggression despite the emergence of a gangster-like ruling stratum, the party bureaucracy.

The [Great Purge](/wiki/Great_Purge "Great Purge") occurred from 1936 to 1938 as a result of growing internal tensions between the critics of Stalin but eventually turned into an all-out cleansing of "anti-Soviet elements".[[36]](#cite_note-:8-36) A majority of those targeted were peasants and minorities, but anarchists and democratic socialist opponents were also targeted for their criticisms of the [severely repressive political techniques](/wiki/Political_repression_in_the_Soviet_Union "Political repression in the Soviet Union") that Stalin used.[[28]](#cite_note-:33-28) Many were executed or sent to [Gulag](/wiki/Gulag "Gulag") prison camps extrajudicially.[[36]](#cite_note-:8-36) It is estimated that during the Great Purge, casualties ranged from 600,000 to over 1 million people.[[36]](#cite_note-:8-36)

> With all the greater frankness can I state how, in my view, the Soviet government should act in case of a fascist upheaval in Germany. In their place, I would, at the very moment of receiving telegraphic news of this event, sign a mobilisation order calling up several age groups. In the face of a mortal enemy, when the logic of the situation points to inevitable war, it would be irresponsible and unpardonable to give that enemy time to establish himself, to consolidate his positions, to conclude alliances ... and to work out the plan to attack.

Trotsky describing the military measures he would have taken in place of Stalin to negate the rise of [Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") in 1932.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Concurrently, fascism was rising across Europe. Initially, during the Comintern's "[third period](/wiki/Third_period "Third period")", Communist parties saw the democratic left as [social fascists](/wiki/Social_fascists "Social fascists"), or as a worse enemy than fascism. The anti-Stalinist left played a major role in the emergence of [anti-fascism](/wiki/Anti-fascism "Anti-fascism") in this period.[[38]](#cite_note-Hyslop-38) The Soviet leadership switched to a [popular front](/wiki/Popular_front "Popular front") policy in 1933, in which Communists were expected to work with liberal and even conservative allies to defend against an expected fascist assault. Although Communists and their [fellow travellers](/wiki/Fellow_travellers "Fellow travellers") in CP-dominated [front organisations](/wiki/Communist_front "Communist front") played a major role in the anti-fascist movement after 1933, [Enzo Traverso](/wiki/Enzo_Traverso "Enzo Traverso") and other historians have argued that the historiography has often obscured the role of the anti-Stalinist left: “it was possible to be both antifascist and anti-Stalinist, and... the fascination exercised by Stalinism at this time over the antifascist intelligentsia was not irresistible."[[39]](#cite_note-Wald-39)

One of the most conflicts of the time was the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"). While the whole left fought alongside the [Republican faction](/wiki/Republican_faction_(Spanish_Civil_War) "Republican faction (Spanish Civil War)"), within it there were sharp conflicts between the Communists, on the one hand, and anarchists, Trotskyists and the [POUM](/wiki/POUM "POUM") (the Spanish affiliate of the International Revolutionary Marxist Centre) on the other.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) Support for the latter became a key issue for the anti-Stalinist left internationally, as exemplified by the [ILP Contingent](/wiki/ILP_Contingent "ILP Contingent") in the [International Brigades](/wiki/International_Brigades "International Brigades"), [George Orwell](/wiki/George_Orwell "George Orwell")'s book *[Homage to Catalonia](/wiki/Homage_to_Catalonia "Homage to Catalonia")*, the periodical *[Spain and the World](/wiki/Spain_and_the_World "Spain and the World")*, and various pamphlets by [Emma Goldman](/wiki/Emma_Goldman "Emma Goldman"), [Rudolf Rocker](/wiki/Rudolf_Rocker "Rudolf Rocker") and others.[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-Høgsbjerg_2013_pp._243–275-44)

Illustrating the role of the anti-Stalinist left in the anti-fascist movement, historian Jonathan Hyslop gives the example of the "Antwerp Group" of former Communist activists in the [International Transport Workers' Federation](/wiki/International_Transport_Workers%27_Federation "International Transport Workers' Federation"), led by [Hermann Knüfken](/wiki/Hermann_Kn%C3%BCfken "Hermann Knüfken"). This group sent fighters to Spain, where they joined an [international militia](/wiki/International_Brigade "International Brigade") linked to the [UGT](/wiki/Uni%C3%B3n_General_de_Trabajadores "Unión General de Trabajadores") union federation, but were expelled by the group’s Communst Party leader, [Hans Beimler](/wiki/Hans_Beimler_(politician) "Hans Beimler (politician)"), over political differences, whereupon they joined the anarchist [Durruti Column](/wiki/Durruti_Column "Durruti Column").[[38]](#cite_note-Hyslop-38) Traverso gives the examples of socialists [Gaetano Salvemini](/wiki/Gaetano_Salvemini "Gaetano Salvemini") (who founded the first clandestine anti-fascist newspaper *[Non mollare](/w/index.php?title=Non_mollare&action=edit&redlink=1 "Non mollare (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non_mollare "it:Non mollare")]* ("Don't Give Up") in January 1925[[45]](#cite_note-pugliese64-45)) and [Carlo Rosselli](/wiki/Carlo_Rosselli "Carlo Rosselli") (who founded the [Giustizia e Libertà](/wiki/Giustizia_e_Libert%C3%A0 "Giustizia e Libertà") anti-fascist group and then fought in Spain as the leader, with [Camillo Berneri](/wiki/Camillo_Berneri "Camillo Berneri") of the [Matteotti Battalion](/wiki/Matteotti_Battalion "Matteotti Battalion"), a mixed volunteer unit of anarchist, [liberal](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism"), socialist and Communist Italians).[[46]](#cite_note-Traverso-46)

In other countries too, non-Communist left parties competed with Stalinism as the same time as they fought the right. The [Three Arrows](/wiki/Three_Arrows "Three Arrows") symbol was adopted by the German [Social Democrats](/wiki/SPD "SPD") to signify this multi-pronged conflict.[[47]](#cite_note-Potthoff-47)

## Mid-century critiques (1939–1953)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anti-Stalinist_left&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Mid-century critiques (1939–1953)")]

See also: [Titoism](/wiki/Titoism "Titoism")

Dissidents in the [Trotskyist](/wiki/Trotskyism "Trotskyism") [Socialist Workers Party](/wiki/Socialist_Workers_Party_(United_States) "Socialist Workers Party (United States)"), witnessing the collaboration of [Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin") and [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") in the invasion and the partition of Poland and the Soviet invasion of the [Baltic states](/wiki/Baltic_states "Baltic states"), argued that the Soviet Union had actually emerged as a new social formation, which was neither capitalist nor socialist. Adherents of that view, espoused most explicitly by [Max Shachtman](/wiki/Max_Shachtman "Max Shachtman") and closely following the writings of [James Burnham](/wiki/James_Burnham "James Burnham") and [Bruno Rizzi](/wiki/Bruno_Rizzi "Bruno Rizzi"), argued that the Soviet [bureaucratic collectivist](/wiki/Bureaucratic_collectivism "Bureaucratic collectivism") regime had in fact entered one of two great imperialist "camps" aiming to wage war to divide the world. The first of the imperialist camps, which Stalin and the Soviet Union were said to have joined as a directly participating ally, was headed by Nazi Germany and included most notably Fascist Italy. In that original analysis, the "second imperialist camp" was headed by England and France, actively supported by the United States.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

Shachtman and his cothinkers argued for the establishment of a broad "[third camp](/wiki/Third_camp "Third camp")" to unite the workers and colonial peoples of the world in revolutionary struggle against the [imperialism](/wiki/Imperialism "Imperialism") of the German–Soviet–Italian and the Anglo–American–French blocs. Shachtman concluded that the Soviet policy was one of imperialism and that the best result for the international working class would be the defeat of the Soviet Union in the course of its military incursions. Conversely, Trotsky argued that a defeat for the Soviet Union would strengthen capitalism and reduce the possibilities for political revolution.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Josip_Broz_Tito_uniform_portrait.jpg/250px-Josip_Broz_Tito_uniform_portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Josip_Broz_Tito_uniform_portrait.jpg)

Tito was a heavy critic of Stalin after their [split in 1948](/wiki/Tito%E2%80%93Stalin_split "Tito–Stalin split").

[Josip Broz Tito](/wiki/Josip_Broz_Tito "Josip Broz Tito") became one of the most prominent leftist critics of Stalin after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The [Communist Party of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Yugoslavia "Communist Party of Yugoslavia") and the policies that were established was originally closely modeled on that of the Soviet Union.[[50]](#cite_note-:9-50) In the eyes of many, "Yugoslavia followed perfectly down the path of Soviet Marxism".[[50]](#cite_note-:9-50) At the start, Tito was even considered "[Stalin](/wiki/Joseph_Stalin "Joseph Stalin")'s most faithful pupil".[[51]](#cite_note-:10-51) However, as the Yugoslavian Communist Party grew in size and power, it became a secondary Communist powerhouse in Europe.[[50]](#cite_note-:9-50) This eventually caused Tito to try to operate independently, which created tensions with Stalin and the Soviet Union.[[50]](#cite_note-:9-50) In 1948, the two leaders [split apart](/wiki/Tito%E2%80%93Stalin_split "Tito–Stalin split") because of Yugoslavian independent foreign policy and ideological differences.[[50]](#cite_note-:9-50)[[51]](#cite_note-:10-51)

Tito and his followers began a political effort to develop a new brand of socialism that would be both [Marxist–Leninist](/wiki/Marxist%E2%80%93Leninist "Marxist–Leninist") in nature yet anti-Stalinist in practice.[[50]](#cite_note-:9-50) The result was the Yugoslav system of socialist [workers' self-management](/wiki/Workers%27_self-management "Workers' self-management").[[50]](#cite_note-:9-50) This led to the philosophy of organizing of every production-related activity in society into "self-managed units".[[50]](#cite_note-:9-50) This came to be known as [Titoism](/wiki/Titoism "Titoism"). Tito was critical of Stalin because he believed Stalin became "un-Marxian".[[50]](#cite_note-:9-50) In the pamphlet titled "On New Roads to Socialism" one of Tito's high ranking aides states:[[50]](#cite_note-:9-50)

> The indictment is long indeed: unequal relations with and exploitation of the other socialist countries, un-Marxian treatment of the role of the leader, inequality in pay greater than in bourgeois democracies, ideological promotion of Great Russian nationalism and subordination of other peoples, a policy of division of spheres of influence with the capitalist world, monopolization of the interpretation of Marxism, the abandonment of all democratic forms ...

Tito disagreed on the primary characteristics that defined Stalin's policy and style of leadership. Tito wanted to form his own version of "pure" socialism without many of the "un-Marxian" traits of [Stalinism](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism").[[51]](#cite_note-:10-51) Tito has also accused Stalinist USSR's hegemonic practices in Eastern Europe and economic exploitation of the Soviet satellite states as [imperialist](/wiki/Social_imperialism "Social imperialism").[[52]](#cite_note-Perović,_2007-52)

Other foreign leftist critics also came about during this time in Europe and America. Some of these critics include [George Orwell](/wiki/George_Orwell "George Orwell"), [H. N. Brailsford](/wiki/H._N._Brailsford "H. N. Brailsford"),[[53]](#cite_note-53) [Fenner Brockway](/wiki/Fenner_Brockway "Fenner Brockway"),[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) the [Young People's Socialist League](/wiki/Young_People%27s_Socialist_League_(1907) "Young People's Socialist League (1907)"), and later [Michael Harrington](/wiki/Michael_Harrington "Michael Harrington"),[[56]](#cite_note-56) and the [Independent Labour Party](/wiki/Independent_Labour_Party "Independent Labour Party") in Britain. There were also several anti-Stalinist socialists in France, including writers such as [Simone Weil](/wiki/Simone_Weil "Simone Weil")[[57]](#cite_note-57) and [Albert Camus](/wiki/Albert_Camus "Albert Camus")[[58]](#cite_note-58) as well as the group around [Marceau Pivert](/wiki/Marceau_Pivert "Marceau Pivert").

In America, [the New York Intellectuals](/wiki/The_New_York_Intellectuals "The New York Intellectuals") around the journals *[New Leader](/wiki/New_Leader "New Leader")*, *[Partisan Review](/wiki/Partisan_Review "Partisan Review"),* and *[Dissent](/wiki/Dissent_(American_magazine) "Dissent (American magazine)")* were among other critics. In general, these figures criticized Soviet Communism as a form of "[totalitarianism](/wiki/Totalitarianism "Totalitarianism") which in some ways mirrored [fascism](/wiki/Fascism "Fascism")".[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-Wilford_2003_pp._15–34-60) A key text for this movement was *[The God That Failed](/wiki/The_God_That_Failed "The God That Failed")*, edited by British socialist [Richard Crossman](/wiki/Richard_Crossman "Richard Crossman") in 1949, featuring contributions by [Louis Fischer](/wiki/Louis_Fischer "Louis Fischer"), [André Gide](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Gide "André Gide"), [Arthur Koestler](/wiki/Arthur_Koestler "Arthur Koestler"), [Ignazio Silone](/wiki/Ignazio_Silone "Ignazio Silone"), [Stephen Spender](/wiki/Stephen_Spender "Stephen Spender") and [Richard Wright](/wiki/Richard_Wright_(author) "Richard Wright (author)"), about their journeys to anti-Stalinism.

## After the death of Stalin (1953–1967)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anti-Stalinist_left&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: After the death of Stalin (1953–1967)")]

Main articles: [De-Stalinization](/wiki/De-Stalinization "De-Stalinization") and [Predictions of the collapse of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Predictions_of_the_collapse_of_the_Soviet_Union "Predictions of the collapse of the Soviet Union")

Following the [death of Joseph Stalin](/wiki/Death_and_state_funeral_of_Joseph_Stalin "Death and state funeral of Joseph Stalin"), many prominent leaders of Stalin's cabinet sought to seize power. As a result, a [Soviet triumvirate](/wiki/Soviet_troika "Soviet troika") was formed between [Lavrenty Beria](/wiki/Lavrenty_Beria "Lavrenty Beria"), [Georgy Malenkov](/wiki/Georgy_Malenkov "Georgy Malenkov"), and [Nikita Khrushchev](/wiki/Nikita_Khrushchev "Nikita Khrushchev"). The primary goal of the new leadership was to ensure stability in the country while leadership positions within the government were sorted out. Some of the new policy implemented that was antithetical of Stalinism included policy that was free from terror, that decentralized power, and collectivized leadership. After this long power struggle within the Soviet government, Nikita Khrushchev came into power. Once in power, Khrushchev was quick to denounce Stalin and his methods of governance.[[61]](#cite_note-:11-61) In a secret speech delivered to the 20th party congress in 1956, Khrushchev was critical of the "cult of personality of Stalin" and his selfishness as a leader:[[61]](#cite_note-:11-61)

> Comrades, the cult of the individual acquired such monstrous size chiefly because Stalin himself, using all conceivable methods, supported the glorification of his own person. This is supported by numerous facts.

Khrushchev also revealed to the congress the truth behind Stalin's methods of repression. In addition, he explained that Stalin had rounded up "thousands of people and sent them into a huge system of political work camps" called [gulags](/wiki/Gulag "Gulag").[[61]](#cite_note-:11-61) The truths revealed in this speech came to the surprise of many, but this fell into the plan of Khrushchev. This speech tainted Stalin's name which resulted in a significant loss of faith in his policy from government officials and citizens.[[61]](#cite_note-:11-61)

There were attempts within the Soviet Union's [satellite states](/wiki/Warsaw_Bloc "Warsaw Bloc") to find a left-wing path that departed from Moscow's directives, met with repression by Soviet-backed governments. In June 1956, the [Polish Army](/wiki/Polish_Land_Forces "Polish Land Forces") violently repressed the [workers' uprising at Poznań](/wiki/1956_Pozna%C5%84_protests "1956 Poznań protests") against the economic policies of the [Polish People's Republic](/wiki/Polish_People%27s_Republic "Polish People's Republic"). The [Hungarian Revolution of 1956](/wiki/Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956 "Hungarian Revolution of 1956") lasted fifteen days before being crushed by Soviet tanks. The repression in Hungary led to further disillusionment with Stalinism globally, and precipitated splits within and departures from Communist parties. In the UK, for example, historian [E.P. Thompson](/wiki/E.P._Thompson "E.P. Thompson"), then a party member, later recalled many calling for an “organized movement of the Marxist anti-Stalinist left" outside the party.[[62]](#cite_note-62) This was the catalyst for the emergence of the [New Left](/wiki/New_Left "New Left").[[63]](#cite_note-:1-63): 285

During this period, known as the [Khrushchev Thaw](/wiki/Khrushchev_Thaw "Khrushchev Thaw") (1956–64), a dissident left emerged in the Soviet Union, including the Vail group in [Leningrad](/wiki/Leningrad "Leningrad") (1956–58), who read texts by anarchists and the Workers Opposition and published “Theses on the Hungarian Revolution” and “The Truth About Hungary,” which emphasized the role of [workers’ councils](/wiki/Workers%E2%80%99_councils "Workers’ councils"); and the [Union of Communards](/w/index.php?title=Union_of_Communards&action=edit&redlink=1 "Union of Communards (page does not exist)") in Leningrad (1960-1965), who wrote pamphlets such as “From the Dictatorship of the Bureaucracy to the Dictatorship of the Proletariat”, drawing on Lenin's *State and Revolution* to criticise Soviet bureaucracy. [Mayakovsky Square](/w/index.php?title=Mayakovsky_Square&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mayakovsky Square (page does not exist)") in Moscow became a key meeting point for such groups from 1958.[[64]](#cite_note-Wainer-64)

During this [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") era, the American non-Communist left (NCL) grew.[[65]](#cite_note-65) The NCL was critical of the continuation of Stalinist Communism because of aspects such as famine and repression,[[6]](#cite_note-:12-6) and as later discovered, the covert intervention of Soviet state interests in the [Communist Party USA](/wiki/Communist_Party_USA "Communist Party USA") (CPUSA).[[66]](#cite_note-:0-66): 31 Members of the NCL were often ex-Communists, such as the historian [Theodore Draper](/wiki/Theodore_Draper "Theodore Draper") whose views shifted from socialism to liberalism, and socialists who became disillusioned with the Communist movement. Anti-Stalinist Trotskyists also wrote about their experiences during this time, such as [Irving Howe](/wiki/Irving_Howe "Irving Howe") and [Lewis Coser](/wiki/Lewis_A._Coser "Lewis A. Coser").[[66]](#cite_note-:0-66): 29–30 These perspectives inspired the creation of the [Congress for Cultural Freedom](/wiki/Congress_for_Cultural_Freedom "Congress for Cultural Freedom") (CCF), as well as international journals like *[Der Monat](/wiki/Melvin_J._Lasky "Melvin J. Lasky")* and *[Encounter](/wiki/Encounter_(magazine) "Encounter (magazine)")*; it also influenced existing publications such as the *[Partisan Review](/wiki/Partisan_Review "Partisan Review")*.[[67]](#cite_note-:13-67) According to [John Earl Haynes](/wiki/John_Earl_Haynes "John Earl Haynes") and [Harvey Klehr](/wiki/Harvey_Klehr "Harvey Klehr"), the CCF was covertly funded by the [Central Intelligence Agency](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency") (CIA) to support intellectuals with pro-democratic and anti-Communist stances.[[66]](#cite_note-:0-66): 66–69 The Communist Party USA lost much of its influence in the first years of the Cold War due to the revelation of Stalinist crimes by Khrushchev.[[68]](#cite_note-68) Although the Soviet Communist Party was no longer officially Stalinist, the Communist Party USA received a substantial subsidy from the USSR from 1959 until 1989, and consistently supported official Soviet policies such as intervention in Hungary and Czechoslovakia. The Soviet funding ended in 1989 when [Gus Hall](/wiki/Gus_Hall "Gus Hall") condemned the market initiatives of [Mikhail Gorbachev](/wiki/Mikhail_Gorbachev "Mikhail Gorbachev").[[69]](#cite_note-69)

From the late 1950s, several European socialist and Communist parties, such as in [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") and [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), shifted away from orthodox Communism which they connected to Stalinism that was in recent history.[[63]](#cite_note-:1-63): 240 [Albert Camus](/wiki/Albert_Camus "Albert Camus") criticized Soviet Communism, while many leftists saw the Soviet Union as emblematic of "[state capitalism](/wiki/State_capitalism "State capitalism")".[[70]](#cite_note-70)

After Stalin's death and the Khrushchev Thaw, study and opposition to Stalinism became a part of historiography. The historian [Moshe Lewin](/wiki/Moshe_Lewin "Moshe Lewin") cautioned not to categorize the entire history of the Soviet Union as Stalinist, but also emphasized that Stalin's bureaucracy had permanently established "bureaucratic absolutism", resembling old monarchy, in the Soviet Union.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

## Castroist critiques (1959–1968)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anti-Stalinist_left&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Castroist critiques (1959–1968)")]

Main article: [Betrayal thesis](/wiki/Betrayal_thesis "Betrayal thesis")

After Fidel Castro's visit to the United States in 1959, various American academics began publishing essays and books on the character of the [Cuban Revolution](/wiki/Cuban_Revolution "Cuban Revolution") and [Fidel Castro](/wiki/Fidel_Castro "Fidel Castro"). Some arguing that Castro was veering away from the goals of the Cuban Revolution, and towards Stalinism. Others argued that the criticisms of Castro were unwarranted.[[72]](#cite_note-72) Throughout 1960, many articles were published in the socialist *[Monthly Review](/wiki/Monthly_Review "Monthly Review")* journal, arguing against any rumored "betrayal" of the Cuban Revolution. These articles were influenced by the writings of socialists [Paul Sweezy](/wiki/Paul_Sweezy "Paul Sweezy") and [Leo Huberman](/wiki/Leo_Huberman "Leo Huberman"), who visited Cuba in 1959.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

In 1961, the historian [Theodore Draper](/wiki/Theodore_Draper "Theodore Draper") famously published in the anti-Stalinist [*Encounter* magazine](/wiki/Encounter_(magazine) "Encounter (magazine)") that [Fidel Castro](/wiki/Fidel_Castro "Fidel Castro") had betrayed the [Cuban Revolution](/wiki/Cuban_Revolution "Cuban Revolution") and could bring international war. The article was passed to [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy"), who considered it before approving the [Bay of Pigs Invasion](/wiki/Bay_of_Pigs_Invasion "Bay of Pigs Invasion").[[74]](#cite_note-74) According to Draper, the Cuban Revolution was a middle class movement for democracy. Castro, after coming to power, began pursuing a wave of land reforms in 1960 and 1961. During this time, Castro drifted away from his original democratic goals. Eventually, Castro heavily integrated Communist officials into his provisional government, and by Draper's conception, Castro had abandoned the democratic goals of the Cuban Revolution, and his own land reform plans of 1960–1961.[[75]](#cite_note-baseball-75)

Draper considered his betrayal thesis to be a criticism of the accounts of socialists like [Paul Sweezy](/wiki/Paul_Sweezy "Paul Sweezy") and [Leo Huberman](/wiki/Leo_Huberman "Leo Huberman") who were sympathetic to Castro. Draper attempted to present a Marxist interpretation of Castroism, that made analogies to Trotskyist conceptions of Stalinism as a betrayer of the [Russian Revolution](/wiki/Russian_Revolution "Russian Revolution").[[76]](#cite_note-76)

Draper's work as a historian of the Cuban Revolution brought him to the attention of the [Hoover Institution on War, Revolution, and Peace](/wiki/Hoover_Institution "Hoover Institution"), an anti-Communist [think tank](/wiki/Think_tank "Think tank") located at [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University").[[77]](#cite_note-TimeObit-77) Draper accepted a Hoover Institution fellowship and remained there until 1968, at which time he departed, ill at ease with the growing conservatism of the institution.[[77]](#cite_note-TimeObit-77)

## Later developments (1968–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anti-Stalinist_left&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Later developments (1968–present)")]

See also: [Soviet dissidents](/wiki/Soviet_dissidents "Soviet dissidents") and [Prague Spring](/wiki/Prague_Spring "Prague Spring")

Anti-Stalinist leftists, influenced by [Western Marxism](/wiki/Western_Marxism "Western Marxism"), continued to organise in the Soviet Union. [Roy Medvedev](/wiki/Roy_Medvedev "Roy Medvedev") published the [samizdat](/wiki/Samizdat "Samizdat") *Political Diary* to influence “party-democrats” in the hopes of reforming the regime. [Elkon Gergieveich Leikin](/w/index.php?title=Elkon_Gergieveich_Leikin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Elkon Gergieveich Leikin (page does not exist)"), a veteran of the anti-Stalinist oppositions of the 1920s, wrote *The Origins of Stalinism*, published by the French Trotskyist [League of Revolutionary Communists](/w/index.php?title=League_of_Revolutionary_Communists&action=edit&redlink=1 "League of Revolutionary Communists (page does not exist)") in the early 1980s. In 1977, the Young Socialists formed at the [Moscow State University](/wiki/Moscow_State_University "Moscow State University"), with [Boris Kagarlitsky](/wiki/Boris_Kagarlitsky "Boris Kagarlitsky") among its members. In the late 1980s, anti-Stalinist leftists formed Trotskyist and anarchist currents in the collapsing USSR.[[64]](#cite_note-Wainer-64)

The fall of the Soviet states briefly led to the revival of the anti-Stalinist left, as Traverso relates:

> At the beginning we were euphoric: “the Berlin Wall is falling down, and this means a German Revolution is coming.” This was the view of Ernest Mandel, for instance: after many decades of passivity and exclusion, the German proletariat would suddenly return in the heart of Europe to accomplish a socialist revolution, which would be the convergence between an anti-capitalist revolution in the West and an anti-bureaucratic, anti-Stalinist revolution in Eastern Europe. Germany was considered the place where these revolutions could merge. Everybody was extremely enthusiastic. Trotskyists, who had always been anti-Stalinist, couldn't help but support this movement.[[78]](#cite_note-h055-78)
